risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Ninety patients were randomized 2:1 to the treatment arm using a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups for disease severity. Groups were stratified according to baseline six minute walk test with a threshold of 300 meters and baseline upright forced vital capacity with a threshold of 55% of the predicted value." Comment: The study reports randomization with a minimization algorithm and stratification, which are systematic allocation methods consistent with a proper random sequence; this is sufficient to judge low risk for sequence generation.

_evidence.method_quote: Ninety patients were randomized 2:1 to the treatment arm using a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups for disease severity (29). Groups were stratified according to baseline six minute walk test with a threshold of 300 meters and baseline upright forced vital capacity with a threshold of 55% of the predicted value.
_evidence.method_summary: Patients were randomized 2:1 using a minimization algorithm, with stratification by baseline 6-minute walk test and upright forced vital capacity.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Ninety patients were randomized 2:1 to the treatment arm using a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups for disease severity" Comment: This describes the randomization/sequence method, but there is no explicit information about how allocation was concealed from the person enrolling participants. No central allocation, sealed opaque numbered envelopes, or other concealment mechanism is reported, so foreknowledge cannot be ruled out but also cannot be confirmed.

_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation/randomization only: "Ninety patients were randomized 2:1 to the treatment arm using a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups for disease severity" and "Groups were stratified according to baseline six minute walk test... and baseline upright forced vital capacity..." but it says nothing about concealment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This study was a post-hoc analysis of cardiovascular parameters in a double-blind, multi-center, randomized controlled trial" and "the control group received a volume matched placebo infusion." Comment: The trial explicitly states that it was double-blind and used a matched placebo infusion, indicating participants and personnel were intended to be blinded. The main outcomes here are predominantly objective cardiovascular measures (ECG/echocardiographic parameters), so knowledge of allocation would be unlikely to materially affect the dominant study outcomes.

_evidence.blinding_quote: This study was a post-hoc analysis of cardiovascular parameters in a double-blind, multi-center, randomized controlled trial evaluating the efficacy and safety of acid alpha-glucosidase enzyme replacement therapy versus placebo in patients with late onset Pompe disease.
_evidence.blinding_method: double-blind; enzyme replacement therapy versus volume matched placebo infusion

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All ECG’s were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment arm. ... All echocardiograms were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment allocation." Comment: The outcome assessor was explicitly blinded for the reported cardiovascular outcomes. The assessed outcomes here are objective ECG and echocardiographic parameters, which are unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation when interpreted by a blinded central core lab. No subjective or patient-reported outcomes are included in this analysis, so the worst-outcome rule does not elevate risk.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: All ECG’s were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment arm. All echocardiograms were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment allocation.

outcomes.label: cardiovascular abnormalities during the study period

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Five (8%) patients in the enzyme replacement therapy group and four (14%) patients in the placebo group did not complete the study. Reasons for not completing the study in the treatment group included the following: hypersensitivity reactions related to the study medication (2), death unrelated to treatment (1), and switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (2). In the control group, the reasons included: adverse event not related to study medication (1), switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (1), and missed 78-week study appointments (2)." Comment: Post-randomization attrition on the analysed 78-week outcome was modest overall (8% in ERT vs 14% placebo) and not extreme; the imbalance is 6 percentage points, below the usual high-risk threshold. Although some discontinuations had outcome-/treatment-related reasons (e.g., hypersensitivity reactions, adverse event), the reported loss is still relatively low and the paper provides explicit per-arm numbers and reasons, supporting low risk rather than unclear.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “We excluded three patients < 17 years of age from the analysis”; “Five (8%) patients in the enzyme replacement therapy group … did not complete the study. Reasons for not completing the study in the treatment group included the following: hypersensitivity reactions related to the study medication (2), death unrelated to treatment (1), and switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (2).”

“We excluded three patients < 17 years of age from the analysis”; “four (14%) patients in the placebo group did not complete the study. In the control group, the reasons included: adverse event not related to study medication (1), switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (1), and missed 78-week study appointments (2).”

_evidence.notes: Efficacy analysis used only those with data at baseline and 78 weeks: “Only those patients with data points both at baseline and at 78 weeks were included in the efficacy analysis.” The attrition numbers above relate to the cardiovascular post-hoc analysis, not the original trial’s primary 6MWT/FVC outcomes.
